content:
# Genesis Chapter 42 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25r–25v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_042_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_042_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 42–43 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0046 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0047

---

*Jacob Sends Ten Brothers to Egypt for Grain (vv. 1–5)*

**42:1** And Jacob saw [וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב] that there was grain [כִּי יֶשׁ-שֶׁבֶר] in Egypt [בְּמִצְרָיִם] — and Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to his sons [אֶל-בָּנָיו]: Why do you look at one another [לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ]?

**42:2** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — I have heard [שָׁמַעְתִּי] that there is grain [כִּי יֶשׁ-שֶׁבֶר] in Egypt [בְּמִצְרָיִם] — go down there [רְדוּ-שָׁמָּה] and buy [וְשִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ] for us from there [מִשָּׁם] — that we may live [וְנִחְיֶה] and not die [וְלֹא נָמוּת].

**42:3** And Joseph's ten brothers [וַיֵּרְדוּ אֲחֵי-יוֹסֵף עֲשָׂרָה] went down [וַיֵּרְדוּ] to buy [לִשְׁבֹּר] grain from Egypt [בָּר מִמִּצְרָיִם].

**42:4** But Benjamin [וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִין] — Joseph's brother [אֲחִי יוֹסֵף] — Jacob did not send [לֹא-שָׁלַח יַעֲקֹב] with his brothers [אֶת-אֶחָיו] — for he said [כִּי אָמַר]: Lest harm befall him [פֶּן-יִקְרָאֶנּוּ אָסוֹן].

**42:5** And the sons of Israel [וַיָּבֹאוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] came to buy [לִשְׁבֹּר] among those who came [בְּתוֹךְ הַבָּאִים] — for the famine [כִּי-הָיָה הָרָעָב] was in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן].

---

*The Dream of the Sheaves Fulfilled — Joseph Recognizes His Brothers (vv. 6–9a)*

**42:6** And Joseph [וְיוֹסֵף] was the governor [הוּא הַשַּׁלִּיט] over the land [עַל-הָאָרֶץ] — he was the one selling [הוּא הַמַּשְׁבִּיר] to all the people of the land [לְכָל-עַם הָאָרֶץ] — and Joseph's brothers came [וַיָּבֹאוּ אֲחֵי יוֹסֵף] and bowed down to him [וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ] with their faces to the ground [אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה].

**42:7** And Joseph saw [וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף] his brothers [אֶת-אֶחָיו] and recognized them [וַיַּכִּירֵם] — but he made himself unrecognizable to them [וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם] and spoke with them harshly [וַיְדַבֵּר אִתָּם קָשׁוֹת] — and he said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם]: From where have you come [מֵאַיִן בָּאתֶם]? And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: From the land of Canaan [מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן] — to buy food [לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל].

**42:8** And Joseph recognized [וַיַּכֵּר יוֹסֵף] his brothers [אֶת-אֶחָיו] but they did not recognize him [וְהֵם לֹא הִכִּרֻהוּ].

**42:9** And Joseph remembered [וַיִּזְכֹּר יוֹסֵף] the dreams [אֵת הַחֲלֹמוֹת] that he had dreamed about them [אֲשֶׁר חָלַם לָהֶם] — and he said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם]: Spies [מְרַגְּלִים] you are [אַתֶּם] — to see the nakedness of the land [לִרְאוֹת אֶת-עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ] you have come [בָּאתֶם].

---

*Joseph Accuses Them as Spies — The Test Proposed (vv. 9b–17)*

**42:10** And they said to him [וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו]: No, my lord [לֹא אֲדֹנִי] — but your servants [וַעֲבָדֶיךָ] have come [בָּאוּ] to buy food [לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל].

**42:11** We are all [כֻּלָּנוּ] sons of one man [בְּנֵי אִישׁ-אֶחָד] — we are [אֲנַחְנוּ] upright men [כֵּנִים] — your servants are not [לֹא-הָיוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ] spies [מְרַגְּלִים].

**42:12** And he said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם]: No [לֹא] — for the nakedness of the land [כִּי-עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ] you have come to see [בָּאתֶם לִרְאוֹת].

**42:13** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Your servants are twelve brothers [שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר עֲבָדֶיךָ אַחִים אֲנַחְנוּ] — sons of one man [בְּנֵי אִישׁ-אֶחָד] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — the youngest [הַקָּטֹן] is with our father [אֶת-אָבִינוּ] today [הַיּוֹם] — and the one is no more [וְהָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ].

**42:14** And Joseph said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף]: It is as I said to you [הוּא אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתִּי אֲלֵכֶם] — saying [לֵאמֹר]: Spies you are [מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם].

**42:15** By this you will be tested [בְּזֹאת תִּבָּחֵנוּ] — by the life of Pharaoh [חֵי פַרְעֹה] — you shall not go out from this place [אִם-תֵּצְאוּ מִזֶּה] unless your youngest brother comes here [כִּי אִם-בְּבוֹא אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן הֵנָּה].

**42:16** Send one from among you [שִׁלְחוּ מִכֶּם אֶחָד] and let him fetch [וְיִקַּח] your brother [אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם] — but you will be kept bound [וְאַתֶּם הֵאָסֵרוּ] — and your words will be tested [וְיִבָּחֲנוּ דִבְרֵיכֶם] whether truth is with you [הַאֱמֶת אִתְּכֶם] — and if not [וְאִם-לֹא] — by the life of Pharaoh [חֵי פַרְעֹה] — surely spies you are [כִּי מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם].

**42:17** And he gathered them [וַיֶּאֱסֹף אֹתָם] into custody [אֶל-מִשְׁמָר] three days [שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים].

---

*Joseph's Revised Proposal — One Stays, Nine Go (vv. 18–20)*

**42:18** And Joseph said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף] on the third day [בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי]: This do and live [זֹאת עֲשׂוּ וִחְיוּ] — the God I fear [אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים אֲנִי יָרֵא].

**42:19** If you are upright men [אִם-כֵּנִים אַתֶּם] — let one of your brothers [אֲחִיכֶם אֶחָד] be bound [יֵאָסֵר] in your house of confinement [בְּבֵית מִשְׁמַרְכֶם] — but you go [וְאַתֶּם לְכוּ] and bring [הָבִיאוּ] the provisions for the hunger of your houses [שֶׁבֶר רַעֲבוֹן בָּתֵּיכֶם].

**42:20** And your youngest brother [וְאֶת-אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן] you shall bring to me [תָּבִיאוּ אֵלַי] — and your words will be verified [וְיֵאָמְנוּ דִבְרֵיכֶם] and you will not die [וְלֹא תָמוּתוּ] — and they did so [וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-כֵן].

---

*The Brothers' Confession of Guilt — Joseph Weeps (vv. 21–24)*

**42:21** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] to one another [אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו]: Truly [אֲבָל] we are guilty [אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ] concerning our brother [עַל-אָחִינוּ] — for we saw [אֲשֶׁר רָאִינוּ] the distress of his soul [אֶת-צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ] when he pleaded with us [בְּהִתְחַנְּנוֹ אֵלֵינוּ] and we did not listen [וְלֹא שָׁמָעְנוּ] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] this distress [בָּאָה אֵלֵינוּ] has come upon us [הַצָּרָה הַזֹּאת].

**42:22** And Reuben answered them [וַיַּעַן רְאוּבֵן אֹתָם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Did I not say to you [הֲלוֹא אָמַרְתִּי אֲלֵכֶם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Do not sin against the child [אַל-תֶּחֶטְאוּ בַיָּלֶד] — but you would not listen [וְלֹא שְׁמַעְתֶּם] — and now also [וְגַם-דָּמוֹ] — behold — his blood [הִנֵּה דָמוֹ] is required [נִדְרָשׁ].

**42:23** And they did not know [וְהֵם לֹא יָדְעוּ] that Joseph understood [כִּי שֹׁמֵעַ יוֹסֵף] — for the interpreter was between them [כִּי הַמֵּלִיץ בֵּינֹתָם].

**42:24** And he turned from them [וַיִּסֹּב מֵעֲלֵיהֶם] and wept [וַיֵּבְךְּ] — and returned to them [וַיָּשָׁב אֲלֵיהֶם] and spoke to them [וַיְדַבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם] — and took Simeon from them [וַיִּקַּח מֵאִתָּם אֶת-שִׁמְעוֹן] and bound him [וַיֶּאֱסֹר אֹתוֹ] before their eyes [לְעֵינֵיהֶם].

---

*The Sacks Loaded — Money Returned — The Brothers Depart (vv. 25–28)*

**42:25** And Joseph commanded [וַיְצַו יוֹסֵף] and they filled [וַיְמַלְאוּ] their vessels [אֶת-כְּלֵיהֶם] with grain [בָּר] and to return [וּלְהָשִׁיב] their silver [כַּסְפֵּיהֶם] each man into his sack [אִישׁ אֶל-שַׂקּוֹ] — and to give them [וְלָתֵת לָהֶם] provisions for the road [צֵדָה לַדָּרֶךְ] — and it was done for them so [וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם כֵּן].

**42:26** And they loaded [וַיִּשְׂאוּ] their grain [אֶת-שִׁבְרָם] upon their donkeys [עַל-חֲמֹרֵיהֶם] and departed from there [וַיֵּלְכוּ מִשָּׁם].

**42:27** And one opened [וַיִּפְתַּח הָאֶחָד] his sack [אֶת-שַׂקּוֹ] to give fodder [לָתֵת מִסְפּוֹא] to his donkey [לַחֲמֹרוֹ] at the lodging place [בַּמָּלוֹן] — and he saw [וַיַּרְא] his silver [אֶת-כַּסְפּוֹ] and behold [וְהִנֵּה-הוּא] it was in the mouth of his bag [בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ].

**42:28** And he said to his brothers [וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-אֶחָיו]: My silver has been returned [הוּשַׁב כַּסְפִּי] — and also behold [וְגַם הִנֵּה] it is in my bag [בְּאַמְתַּחְתִּי] — and their heart failed [וַיֵּצֵא לִבָּם] and they trembled [וַיֶּחֶרְדוּ] to one another [אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו] saying [לֵאמֹר]: What is this [מַה-זֹּאת] that God has done [עָשָׂה אֱלֹהִים לָנוּ]?

---

*The Brothers Report to Jacob (vv. 29–34)*

**42:29** And they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to Jacob their father [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם] to the land of Canaan [אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן] and they reported to him [וַיַּגִּידוּ לוֹ] all that had happened to them [אֵת כָּל-הַקֹּרֹת אֹתָם] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**42:30** The man who is lord of the land [דִּבֶּר הָאִישׁ אֲדֹנֵי הָאָרֶץ] spoke with us harshly [אִתָּנוּ קָשׁוֹת] and treated us [וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָנוּ] as spies of the land [כִּמְרַגְּלִים אֶת-הָאָרֶץ].

**42:31** And we said to him [וַנֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו]: We are upright men [כֵּנִים אֲנָחְנוּ] — we are not spies [לֹא הָיִינוּ מְרַגְּלִים].

**42:32** We are twelve brothers [שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר אֲנַחְנוּ אַחִים] — sons of our father [בְּנֵי אָבִינוּ] — the one is no more [הָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ] — and the youngest [וְהַקָּטֹן] is today with our father [הַיּוֹם אֶת-אָבִינוּ] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן].

**42:33** And the man who is lord of the land [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלֵינוּ אִישׁ אֲדֹנֵי הָאָרֶץ] said to us [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלֵינוּ]: By this I will know [בְּזֹאת אֵדַע] that you are upright men [כִּי כֵנִים אַתֶּם] — leave one of your brothers [אֲחִיכֶם אֶחָד] with me [הַנִּיחוּ אִתִּי] — and take [וְאֶת-רַעֲבוֹן בָּתֵּיכֶם קְחוּ] the provisions for the hunger of your houses [רַעֲבוֹן בָּתֵּיכֶם] and go [וָלֵכוּ].

**42:34** And bring [וְהָבִיאוּ] your youngest brother [אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן] to me [אֵלַי] — and I will know [וְאֵדְעָה] that you are not spies [כִּי לֹא מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם] but that you are upright men [כִּי כֵנִים אַתֶּם] — and your brother I will give back to you [אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם אֶתֵּן לָכֶם] — and in the land [וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ] you may trade freely [תִּסְחָרוּ].

---

*The Silver Found in All the Sacks — Jacob's Lament (vv. 35–38)*

**42:35** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] as they were emptying their sacks [הֵם מְרִיקִים שַׂקֵּיהֶם] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה-אִישׁ] each man's bundle of silver [צְרוֹר-כַּסְפּוֹ] was in his sack [בְּשַׂקּוֹ] — and they saw [וַיִּרְאוּ] the bundles of their silver [אֶת-צְרֹרוֹת כַּסְפֵּיהֶם] — they and their father [הֵם וַאֲבִיהֶם] — and they feared [וַיִּירָאוּ].

**42:36** And Jacob their father [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם] said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם]: You have bereaved me [אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם] — Joseph is no more [יוֹסֵף אֵינֶנּוּ] and Simeon is no more [וְשִׁמְעוֹן אֵינֶנּוּ] — and Benjamin you would take [וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִין תִּקָּחוּ] — upon me have all these things fallen [עָלַי הָיוּ כֻלָּנָה].

**42:37** And Reuben said [וַיֹּאמֶר רְאוּבֵן] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו] saying [לֵאמֹר]: My two sons you may kill [אֶת-שְׁנֵי בָנַי תָּמִית] if I do not bring him back to you [אִם-לֹא אֲבִיאֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ] — give him into my hand [תְּנָה אֹתוֹ עַל-יָדִי] and I will return him to you [וַאֲנִי אֲשִׁיבֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ].

**42:38** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: My son shall not go down [לֹא-יֵרֵד בְּנִי] with you [עִמָּכֶם] — for his brother is dead [כִּי-אָחִיו מֵת] and he alone remains [וְהוּא לְבַדּוֹ נִשְׁאָר] — and if harm befalls him [וּקְרָאָהוּ אָסוֹן] on the road [בַּדֶּרֶךְ] upon which you go [אֲשֶׁר תֵּלְכוּ-בָהּ] — then you would bring down my gray hair [וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי] in sorrow [בְּיָגוֹן] to Sheol [שְׁאוֹלָה].

---

## Philological Notes

### 42:1 — לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ (lammah tittra'u)
"Why do you look at one another?" The verb רָאָה (ra'ah) in the hithpael/reflexive stem (תִּתְרָאוּ) carries the sense of looking at one another, staring at each other in helplessness. Some read a note of reproof — "Why are you standing around gazing at each other?" — but the underlying force is a picture of paralysis in the face of famine. Jacob sees grain available in Egypt; his sons have not moved. The LXX renders ἵνα τί ῥᾳθυμεῖτε ("why are you slack/idle?"), interpreting the idiom as passive inaction rather than mutual gazing.

### 42:6 — וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה (vayyishtachavu-lo appayim artsah)
"And they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground." This is the exact fulfillment of Joseph's first dream in 37:7: "and behold, your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to my sheaf" (וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ לַאֲלֻמָּתִי). The narrator has waited five chapters for this moment. The verb שָׁחָה (shacha) in the hishtaphel stem is the standard prostration verb — full-body obeisance with face to the ground. The addition of אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה ("faces to the ground") heightens the completeness of the prostration. Dream 37:9 (sun, moon, eleven stars bowing) still awaits fulfillment — Benjamin and Jacob have not yet bowed. The reader who knows 37:7 feels the moment before Joseph does.

### 42:7 — וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם (vayyitnakker aleihem)
"And he made himself unrecognizable to them." The root נ-כ-ר (n-k-r) in the hithpael stem means "to make oneself a stranger, to disguise oneself." This is the same root used in the painful scene of 37:32 — "הַכֶּר-נָא" ("recognize, please!") — when the brothers brought Joseph's blood-soaked coat to Jacob, forcing him to recognize it. And again in 38:25 — Tamar sends Judah's pledge saying "הַכֶּר-נָא" to the man who fathered her child. Now the verb reverses: Joseph, the recognized one, makes himself the unrecognized. The wordplay is poignant — the brothers once forced a false recognition upon Jacob; now the true subject of that episode refuses recognition to them.

### 42:9 — מְרַגְּלִים (meraglim)
"Spies." The root is רָגַל (ragal), related to רֶגֶל (regel, "foot") — one who "walks about" clandestinely. The term appears in the Num 13 spy narrative for the twelve spies sent to scout Canaan. Joseph's accusation in Genesis 42 is clearly a constructed pretext — he knows they are not spies — but the charge serves his purpose of detaining them without revealing himself. The LXX renders κατάσκοποι ("scouts/spies") throughout.

### 42:9 — עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ (ervat ha'arets)
"The nakedness of the land." עֶרְוָה (ervah) means "nakedness, exposure, vulnerability" — typically used of sexual nakedness in Leviticus, but here deployed metaphorically for the military exposure or vulnerable points of a land. The accusation is that the brothers came to identify weak points in Egypt's defenses. LXX renders τὰ ἴχνη τῆς χώρας ("the tracks/footprints of the country"), reading it as reconnaissance.

### 42:21 — אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ (ashamim anachnu)
"We are guilty." This is the first explicit confession of guilt in the Joseph narrative — and the brothers make it to one another, not yet to Joseph or to God. The adjective/participle אָשֵׁם (asham) is the same root as the אָשָׁם (asham) guilt-offering in Leviticus. The verse is one of the most theologically significant in the Joseph cycle: the brothers spontaneously connect their present suffering with their past crime against Joseph. The grammar is emphatic — "guilty we are" — with the pronoun אֲנַחְנוּ placed at the end for emphasis. This verse is the moral hinge of the entire Joseph story.

### 42:21 — בְּהִתְחַנְּנוֹ אֵלֵינוּ (behitchanno eleinu)
"When he pleaded/besought mercy from us." The hithpael of חָנַן (chanan, "to be gracious") means "to seek favor, to entreat, to plead." The word is related to the divine attribute חֵן (chen, grace) and to the formula אֶחְנֶּךָּ / חָנּוּן ("I will be gracious"). The verse is a painful reversal: Joseph's brothers, now pleading (implicitly) before the lord of Egypt, remember how Joseph once pleaded with them. The memory of his pleas has been suppressed for two decades but surfaces precisely when they themselves are vulnerable. Note: Genesis 37 records no verbal pleas from Joseph at the pit — this detail emerges only here, twenty years later in the brothers' conscience.

### 42:22 — וְגַם-דָּמוֹ הִנֵּה נִדְרָשׁ (vegam-damo hinneh niddrash)
"And now also his blood is required." The verb דָּרַשׁ (darash) in the niphal means "to be sought, to be required, to be called to account." The phrase echoes the blood-vengeance theology of Gen 9:5 ("your blood I will require / אֶדְרֹשׁ"). Reuben's statement is doubly ironic: (1) Joseph is not dead, so there is no blood guilt in the absolute sense; (2) Reuben had tried to rescue Joseph (37:22, 29-30) and this is his way of saying "I told you so." His protest, though righteous, offers no comfort to the group.

### 42:23 — הַמֵּלִיץ (hammelits)
"The interpreter." The word מֵלִיץ (melits) is from לוּץ (luts) — "to speak in riddles, to be an interpreter, to mediate." Joseph has been using an interpreter to speak with his brothers, even though he understands everything they say. The dramatic irony is acute: the brothers speak freely in Joseph's presence, not knowing he understands Hebrew, confessing their guilt. The LXX renders ὁ ἑρμηνεύων ("the one interpreting"), standard terminology for a professional translator.

### 42:24 — וַיֵּבְךְּ (vayyevk)
"And he wept." This is Joseph's first weeping in the narrative — a brief, private moment before he turns back to his brothers. The root בָּכָה (bakah) will appear five more times in the Joseph story (43:30; 45:2, 14–15; 46:29; 50:1), each time marking a moment of profound emotional intensity. Here Joseph turns away (וַיִּסֹּב) before weeping — he cannot let his brothers see. The choice of Simeon as the hostage is likely deliberate: Simeon is the second-born, and in 37:19-20 he may have been the instigator of the plan to kill Joseph (cf. the later pairing of Simeon and Levi in ch. 34 and 49:5-7).

### 42:38 — שְׁאוֹלָה (she'olah)
"To Sheol." שְׁאוֹל (Sheol) is the Hebrew underworld — the place of the dead, the shadowy realm where all the departed go. The directional suffix -ָה (ah, "toward, downward to") emphasizes the descent: to bring down his gray hair to Sheol. Jacob uses this same phrase in 37:35 when he believed Joseph was torn by a wild animal: "I will go down to Sheol mourning to my son." Now he uses it again — a father who has already mourned one son as dead, now fearing to lose another. The phrase "my gray hair [שֵׂיבָה] in sorrow [בְּיָגוֹן]" is an image of a grief-broken old man; his white hair symbolizes the accumulated years of suffering. LXX renders εἰς ᾅδου ("to Hades"), using the standard Greek equivalent.

### 42:38 — וְהוּא לְבַדּוֹ נִשְׁאָר (vehu levaddo nish'ar)
"And he alone remains." The phrase echoes Jacob's self-understanding after he believed Joseph was dead. He now has one remaining son of his beloved Rachel — Benjamin. The word לְבַדּוֹ ("alone, only") carries particular poignancy: Jacob himself has felt alone since the loss of Rachel and then Joseph. Benjamin is the last thread connecting him to Rachel. The irony the reader knows is that Joseph is not dead — but Jacob does not know this.

---

## LXX Comparison Table (Vaticanus, pp. 42–43)

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus Greek) | Significant Divergence |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 42:1 | לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ — "why do you look at one another?" | ἵνα τί ῥᾳθυμεῖτε — "why are you slack/idle?" | LXX interprets the mutual gazing as slackness/inaction; MT pictures helpless staring |
| 42:6 | וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה — "bowed down to him faces to the ground" | προσεκύνησαν αὐτῷ ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν — "prostrated themselves with face to the ground" | Identical in substance; full prostration preserved |
| 42:7 | וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם — "he made himself unrecognizable to them" | ἠλλοτριοῦτο ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν — "he was estranged from them" | LXX uses ἀλλοτριόω ("to estrange/alienate"); MT's hithpael of n-k-r is more active disguise |
| 42:9 | עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ — "nakedness of the land" | τὰ ἴχνη τῆς χώρας — "the tracks of the land" | LXX renders with military reconnaissance idiom; MT's "nakedness" is more vulnerable/exposed |
| 42:13 | וְהָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ — "and the one is no more" | ὁ δὲ εἷς οὐχ ὑπάρχει — "but the one does not exist" | Essentially identical; LXX uses ὑπάρχω for MT's אֵינֶנּוּ |
| 42:15 | חֵי פַרְעֹה — "by the life of Pharaoh" | νὴ τὴν ὑγίειαν Φαραω — "by the health of Pharaoh" | LXX avoids a literal oath formula; "health/welfare" (ὑγίεια) for MT's "life" |
| 42:18 | אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים אֲנִי יָרֵא — "the God I fear" | τὸν θεὸν ἐγὼ φοβοῦμαι — "God I fear" | Identical; Joseph invokes divine fear as the ground for his changed terms |
| 42:21 | אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ — "we are guilty" | ἐν ἁμαρτίᾳ γάρ ἐσμεν — "for we are in sin" | LXX uses ἁμαρτία ("sin") for MT's אָשֵׁם (guilt/culpability); shifts from juridical guilt to moral sin |
| 42:22 | וְגַם-דָּמוֹ הִנֵּה נִדְרָשׁ — "and his blood is required" | καὶ τὸ αἷμα αὐτοῦ ἐκζητεῖται — "and his blood is being sought out" | LXX ἐκζητέω ("seek out earnestly") for MT darash niphal; essentially equivalent |
| 42:23 | הַמֵּלִיץ בֵּינֹתָם — "the interpreter was between them" | ὁ ἑρμηνεύων ἀνὰ μέσον αὐτῶν — "the interpreter in their midst" | Identical in substance |
| 42:25 | וּלְהָשִׁיב כַּסְפֵּיהֶם אִישׁ אֶל-שַׂקּוֹ — "to return their silver each man into his sack" | ἀποδοῦναι τὸ ἀργύριον ἑκάστου εἰς τὸν σάκκον αὐτοῦ — "to give back each man's silver into his sack" | Identical in substance |
| 42:28 | וַיֵּצֵא לִבָּם — "their heart failed / went out" | ἐξέστη δὲ ἡ καρδία αὐτῶν — "their heart was astonished/stood outside itself" | LXX uses ἐξίστημι ("to be beside oneself, astonished"); MT's "heart went out" is more visceral panic |
| 42:36 | אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם — "you have bereaved me" | ἐμὲ ἠτεκνώσατε — "you have made me childless" | LXX uses ἀτεκνόω ("to make childless"); MT uses שִׁכֵּל (piel of shakal, "to bereave, to make childless of children") — equivalent |
| 42:38 | שְׁאוֹלָה — "to Sheol" | εἰς ᾅδου — "to Hades" | Standard equivalence; LXX consistently renders Sheol as Hades throughout Genesis |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 42 is the opening movement of the resolution arc of the Joseph cycle, spanning chapters 42–45. The chapter is constructed around a series of reversals and ironies that operate simultaneously on three levels: narrative, theological, and psychological.

**Narrative architecture:** The chapter divides into four panels — (1) the descent to Egypt and the dream fulfilled (vv. 1–9a); (2) the spy accusation and the test (vv. 9b–24); (3) the return journey and the money in the sacks (vv. 25–28); (4) the brothers' report and Jacob's lament (vv. 29–38). Each panel ends with a moment of heightened emotion: the dream's fulfillment, Joseph's first weeping, the brothers' trembling at the inn, and Jacob's devastating speech.

**The dream fulfillment (v. 6):** The bowing of the ten brothers over grain (שֶׁבֶר, shever) directly echoes 37:7 where the sheaves (אֲלֻמּוֹת, alumot) bowed. The narrator presents this without comment — the reader is expected to remember. Dream one of Joseph is now fulfilled; dream two (37:9) — involving eleven stars and the sun and moon — awaits chapters 43–45 and ultimately 46–47.

**The reversal of the recognition motif (הַכֵּר-נָא):** In 37:32, the brothers presented Joseph's coat to Jacob with the callous words "recognize, please — is this your son's coat?" In 38:25, Tamar sent Judah's pledge saying "recognize, please — whose are these?" Now in 42:7–8, Joseph sees and recognizes (וַיַּכִּירֵם) but makes himself unrecognizable (וַיִּתְנַכֵּר). The root נ-כ-ר has been running through the narrative as a thread of false and withheld recognition; it will culminate in the great recognition scene of 45:1–3.

**The confession of guilt (v. 21):** The brothers' speech to one another is one of the most psychologically realistic moments in biblical narrative. They do not merely acknowledge a past action — they articulate that Joseph's suffering was visible to them (they saw the distress of his soul), that he actively appealed to them (he besought us), and that they chose not to listen. This threefold structure — seeing, being asked, refusing — constitutes full moral culpability. The text invites the reader to feel both their belated remorse and the inadequacy of private guilt that never reaches the one who was wronged.

**Jacob's speech (v. 36):** Jacob's lament is a threefold loss — Joseph, Simeon, Benjamin — expressed as a triple אֵינֶנּוּ pattern: "Joseph is no more / Simeon is no more / Benjamin you would take." The syntax isolates each name. His final word is Sheol: Jacob has been living, since 37:35, as a man with one foot already in the grave. The chapter ends not in resolution but in paralysis — Jacob refuses to send Benjamin, and yet without Benjamin there is no resolution, no food, and no future.
